How you looked into vape pens with wax carts? The vapor they produce is way different than using a bong or joint, and she may be more into that. Alternatively, have you ever heard of D8 CBD? D9 THC is the compound that makes weed federally illegal, and is what gives you the psychoactive effects. It is the most common form on THC in weed. However, D8 THC is also a compound in weed, just in much smaller concentration. It gives you a more “body high” but you still feel high. CBD stores extract this D8 THC from weed to use in D8 CBD stuff. Local CBD shops will sell this, in flower form, in prerolled joint form (both of which are regular plain CBD weed that’s gets coated in a D8 coating) or what I use, which is D8 in the form of vape carts. I have a 510 vape battery ( 25$) and I get carts from my local CBD store ( 40$). Best of all, it’s completely legal! D8 is sold in basically all US states, since D8 isn’t federally illegal. I use D8 mostly (I don’t have a med card yet for my state although I’ll get one eventually). Your partner may be okay with D8 smoking since it’s classified as CBD. May be a good starting point for you two. I hope that D8 rant makes sense. Feel free to comment with questions if you have any. I wish you luck!

I have PTSD and anxiety, which is why I smoke. Indica and indica dominant hybrids tend to make me panic, have nightmares, get more anxious, and be more likely to have mild hallucinations. Even if they aren't scary, the fact that the wall suddenly turned purple for a few seconds for no reason freaks me out enough on it's own! lol. I have tips to get through it! First, I have an app on my phone called "antistress - relaxation toys" with a bunch of simple sensory toys you can mess with. It's a good idea to have a couple real sensory toys too, like a squeeze ball, or some glitter and baby oil in a clear plastic bottle. These are great to give you something simple to focus on besides your thoughts. Deep breathing excersizes are good too. Take four deep and slow breaths. Breathe in while counting to two, breathe out while counting to four. Practice the rule of five: 1- find something in the room that is far from you, and blue. 2: find something in the room that smells really nice. 3: listen to your surroundings, and see if you can name something that sounds pleasant, even if you have to play music to find it. 4: find something in the room that feels nice to touch like slime putty, a glass bead, a soft fabric, a pet's fur, or maybe your hair or gently massaging your own sore muscles. 5: find something that tastes nice and savor it a little. Can you describe what makes it taste so great? (My go-to is dum-dum lollipops because they're cheap, I like them, not a ton of calories to eat one or two, and they have a stick so I'm less likely to choke on it. My ability to not choke reverts to a 2 year old child's when I'm stoned lol) If all else fails, I keep some good quality CBD tincture or buds handy, so that I can take it if I had too much weed and can't handle it. Larger amounts of CBD can counteract THC and make it so you don't feel high anymore. I keep the tinctures in the fridge so they last so they last a while. I get the tinctures here: And flower I buy from a local farm that grows it comercially, but you can probably find it online since it's legal in many places that THC containing marijuana is not.

The more expensive ones are usually the higher quality ones. They are usually from indoor grows, fresher and have a higher THC, CBD and other cannabinoid/terpenes level which greatly increases their therapeutic value as well as their smoothness when smoked. THC just gets you high by itself. You need to be looking at the levels of the other cannabinoids and terpenes if you are actually looking for any medical or therapeutic benefit. In Oklahoma at least, sometimes you can buy a preroll that used the trim from the same high quality top shelf flower, but a bit cheeper if you want to see the difference between the top and bottom shelf products. In a nut shell, don't just look for the cheapest flower or the highest THC level. It's kind of like hot sauce, just because it has a high scoville scale number doesn't make it a good hot sauce. It's the difference between a $3 box of cigars and a $50 single cigar. All made of tobacco, but one is a much higher quality and better smoke.

No problem at all. I’m going to assume that the reason vaping was suggested to you, is due to the health concerns of smoking. Some states until recently, like New York, didn’t even allow you to buy flower. You could only get the oil. That’s changed now, but it’s funny how the states and the doctors have different opinions. Perfect example. I had a major knee surgery last year and they had to take down my medical history. On the report, it stated medication: smokes cannabis. Immediately after, it stated in my problem list. Problem List: VAPES cannabis oil. Why? Because the doctors know that the minor amounts of tar and smoke you’re putting in your lungs, is nowhere near worth the risk of some of the potential additives that people are putting into Vape oils. With that said, if the oil comes from a reputable place, I see the oil to have a higher medicinal value because you’re not inhaling the smoke. Vaping bud or oil can both provide adequate pain relief. If you go the oil route, make sure you are getting a “full spectrum” product. Distillate is like a vitamin c pill, where as full spec is the whole orange. The reason I say to use a full spectrum oil, is that all the cannabinoids, Terpenes, etc. found in the plant, work together to create an entourage effect on your endocannabinoid system. Kind of like how you switch back-and-forth between Tylenol and ibuprofen to get better effects. You would most likely also benefit from some sort of tincture or edible to accompany your Vape. Vaping the right cultivars can provide fantastic pain relief almost immediately, in addition to helping with some of even the most severe PTSD. With that said, vaping is not super long lasting and may only provide you relief for a couple of hours. I buy vaping and taking an edible shortly there after, the Vape kicks in right away to help with the immediate pain and PTSD. whereas the edible will take anywhere from 45 minutes to 2 hours to kick in, but will usually last 6-8 hours. Are for most people with pain, I usually also recommend a topical. However in your case, probably best to avoid that option. I have found at the topicals work fantastic for external muscle and joint pain, but don’t do much for pain if it is not external. Not saying it doesn’t do some thing, just not worth it if you have to buy it from a dispensary. But just deciding to vape is not the end of your search. You still need to determine which cultivars are going to be best for your needs. As discussed before, the cannabinoids and Turpines work together. I don’t believe in the whole sativa versus Indica one being sleepy and one being daytime… Etc. Why? Because of history. Sativas and Indicas just originated from different areas of the world and the original differentiation was due to plant structure and flowering time. Why did the sativa‘s get the rap for daytime and Indica‘s for night time? A couple of reasons. Sativas take much longer to flower, so if you harvest one at nine weeks, it very well may be very immature still. This creates a racy head buzz. Indica take less time to flower. But, many growers will push an Indica an extra week or two to help increase yield. This pushes the plant past the point of maturity and the THC starts to degrade into CBN. The immaturity of the sativa coupled with Terpenes like Limonene, provide a boost of energy. The over mature Indica combined with terpenes such as Linalool and Myrcene, is where the deep relaxing couchlock comes from. In your situation, if you’re going to a dispensary, I would suggest looking at their “hybrid“ options. Do you want to find an option that is well-rounded for your needs. Everyone is different and you’ll have to do some trial and error define what works best for you. But given my experience with the patients I’ve worked with, a good place to start would be: Daytime - 10-15% THC, at least 1% CBD, some CBG if you can find a cultivar with it, Limonene and Myrcene dominant. Nighttime - 15-20% THC, 2.5% or more CBD, some CBN if you can find a cultivar with it, linalool and Myrcene dominant If your options are limited, say you’re using a plug and he only has one option for you, you can try to make that work for both daytime and nighttime by changing the temperature of your Vape. When vaping flower, you can Vape at the highest setting, to encourage some of the THC to convert to CBN during consumption. This will add to the pain management and provide additional ability for you to sleep at night. I am personally a medical patient for PTSD, anxiety, OCPD, both rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis, and high functioning autism. Coughing up anything that isn't just mucous and immune cells is generally a bad sign, your lungs are trying to clear stuff out because they're irritated. This isn't uncommon in daily smokers of any organic matter, and it is probably just a mix of lipids (plant oils) and tar. Wouldn't show up on an x-ray either. The good news is you're young, so your body's healing and detoxification processes are at their most effective. I've seen numerous reports over the years of people saying there was an increase in coughing up the 'black crap' when they quit. This is probably recovery of the cilia - tiny hair-like structures that line your airway and throat to help expel mucus thickened by immune response to pathogens or foreign matter - like tar. If you wish to continue using the herb for its psychological and medical benefits, I always advocate for alternative methods of administration. Oral/sublingual oils and dry herb vaping are the most common healthier alternatives. You'll thank yourself when you're older for kicking the combustion habit. There are many testimonials about this, particularly from the UK because our legal medical cannabis flower is only prescribed to be vaporised. Check out the UK medical cannabis or vaporents subs for info.

Vaping is the best way to take your CBD for instant effects and getting the most bioavailability. It will bypass using your liver to breakdown the CBD to enter it into the bloodstream via the alveoli in the lungs. Tinctures use MCT oil to help bypass the digestive tract to help save time and more bioavailability of the CBD by sending it more directly to the liver to be processed and then entered into the bloodstream. Edibles are the slowest time and lowest bioavailability for CBD effects. The CBD has to go through the digestive tract first, then to the liver and then to the bloodstream. The digestive tract will heavily destroy things like cannabinoids and vitamins, so the full amount you take in will not be the same amount that finally reaches your liver and then your bloodstream. Edibles are the worst way to get the most out of your cannabinoids! I personally feel they are a waste of time and money. I liken it to the bright yellow pee you get after taking a bunch of vitamins...you are just peeing out a bunch of unused minerals and money. THC is a good example of this effect. 2g will not be enough for 6 people. I usually do 7 grams for 1 batch of brownies (1 box of brownies in a 9x13 pan). If they’re new to edibles I’d do 3.5 grams per box. These recipes use oil so it makes it easy. 1- decarb your weed. Set the oven at 240F or below. Grind your weed and place into an aluminum foil pocket (this has worked best for me). Bake for 20 minutes/ flip/ bake for 20 minutes again. 2- remove from the oven. Let it cool COMPLETELY. Measure the oil out for the recipe you’ll be baking. 3- place baked weed and oil on a double boiler on the stove. One pot of water on low/ medium heat (just enough for steam), with a bowl on top for the oil and the weed. Do not use directly heat of the stove. This will take 6-8 hours. The longer you double boil, the more potent. 4- strain the weed from the oil. Use the oil to bake the brownies. 5- eat and enjoy. Happy baking!

Yeah honestly I’d suggest just buying a full spectrum CBD cartridge if you want to vape CBD. The cartridges are much more concentrated. This is designed basically for people who use vape E-cigs as an alternative. It’s rather diluted at 1000mg per 30ml bottle. For a comparison, most vape carts will contain 1ml and are typically around 60-80% CBD. So a 1ml CBD vape cart will contain around 600-800 mg. This bottle is 30ml and contains 1000mg, that’s like 3-4% CBD by volume. So you’re gonna need to vape a lot more of this than you would with an actual CBD cartridge. It depends on the person, but a much better option for CBD intake would be dosing a CBD oil orally, imho. Or if you wanted to vape, picking up a dry herb vaporizer and buying CBD dominant hemp flower. You could also buy CBD concentrate and use a dab pen, or dab rig if you wanted. Most people who use CBD oil orally will take anywhere from 20-500mg daily. Most will start around 10-20mg and work their way up, till they find an effective dose. I personally use all these methods. I take CBD oil doses twice a day, between 100-200mg total daily. Then I also enjoy vaping hemp flower. I buy both CBD and CBG dominant hemp flower from online vendors. Then I consume like one would marijuana. Both hemp and marijuana are both cannabis, just the legal distinction is hemp is any cannabis with less than .3% THC by weight. So I vape it in a dry herb vape, smoke joints, out of a bowl or bong, make edibles, etc. I use around .3-1g of flower a day. It’s the most effective way to use CBD in my opinion. The effects are much more pronounced than something like a CBD cartridge. I also make my own CBD cartridges. I’ll buy CBD distillate online, then add terpenes I’ve bought online as well, and fill Ccell style 510 cartridges that work with any 510 threaded battery. They work but not nearly as well as the flower or CBD oil orally. Idk how familiar you are with CBD, or it’s use, but these vape juices are more of a novelty/replacement for nicotine targeted at people who already use E-juice. The ingredients will be exactly like typical E-juice, they just replace the nicotine content with CBD isolate. So it’ll contain PG/VG, food flavorings for taste, and then CBD isolate instead of the nicotine. So unless you already vape E-juice, I’d suggest one of the other routes I listed above. You’ll get much better effects, and won’t have to take a ton of hits for a decent dose.

For perspective: In Michigan right now all adults can possess up to 2.5 ounces of cannabis on their person or in their car (10oz if locked in the trunk) up to 15 grams of extracts, grow 12 plants with no oversight or restrictions, and most former misdemeanors and felonies for cannabis are now civil infractions or not a crime at all. Proposition 1 is one of the worlds most liberal recreational cannabis legalization laws. There are recreational dispensaries as well as home cannabis delivery services pretty much everywhere here. All this has lead to a huge overabundance of supply resulting in the highest quality cannabis of 25-30% THC selling for $100 an ounce on the black market. Also the liquor stores and convenience stores as well as some gas stations sell marijuana products such as Delta 9 and HHC cannabis flower, pre rolls, wax, oils, and cartridges for vapes. Many low THC CBD products including all that and much more are also sold at many establishments. The overall effect is that profits for criminal enterprises and large commercial growers are on a massive decline which is great in my opinion.

Broad spectrum CBD oil is without THC and the Full Spectrum is with THC it is 0.2% in the UK and can be in various ratios in the United States or Amsterdam etc. What kind of full spectrum were you having? Typically a CBD:THC ratio of 20:1 = High CBD low THC is the best way to start low and go slow or choose a 0.2% Full spectrum oil. It sounds like 40 mg is too high for you. in the Harvard study on anxiety, they are testing 25 mg of full spectrum CBD and seeing a higher quality of life outcomes than the next Brazilian study (below). The benefit is in the entourage effect and the fact that you get a range of medicines in one plant vs 1 medicine like isolate. In the Brazilian study on anxiety, they used CBD isolate. They found the sweet spot or effective dose to be 300 mg of CBD. This is typical with isolate, far higher doses are required, it's harder to dial in the effective dose and can mix more poorly with medications at higher doses. So definitely worth having your doctor on board and monitoring outcomes. Remember CBD is an adaptogen so can take 1-3 weeks to build up in the system, we are also seeing many new studies that show taking CBD more than once a day supports its adaptogenic properties and improves results. If I were aiming for 25 mg a day or 50 mg a day I would take 12.5 mg and 12.5 mg or 25 mg and 25 mg. This would also be a great way to up titrate the dose slowly to see the difference. The Academy of Medical Cannabis (TAOMC) reviews all the medical literature and suggests full spectrum can be 6-10 times more potent so always start low go slow. Actually, the above two studies highlight the 10 x difference: 25 mg and 300 mg. Both these studies can be found in The Essential Guide to CBD by project CBD and readers digest.

By saying this, I don't mean that you should discart the CBD content. Most people don't value terpenes and those are the really difference makers on the desired effects, ence the difference between strains, even in THC rich cannabis, that's why you can find thousands and thousands of different strains. Terpenes are, in a very short version explanation, like essential oils that cannabis share with other flora type plants and fruits such as Rosemary, pines, origanno, strawberries, mangos and many others. It's sad that producers do not test terpenes levels on their flowers like some do in the US. Terpenes give the cannabis it's properties, whether it's a strain that makes you fuzzy, a strain that makes you sleep or that makes you laugh your ass off. You can have a strain that has 10% THC high in terpenes and a 20% THC low in terpenes and I can practically guarantee you that the lowest amount THC strain will probably give you better, stronger effects, besides the flavour. For anxiety, the best terpene is Limonene, which is also present in citrus fruits such as lemons, oranges, etc, and that's the one that could really help improve the fight against anxiety (ex.strains know THC/CBD- Lemon Haze, OG Kush). Linalool is also excellent choice for both anxiety and pain (Ex.strains - Blue Dream, Zkittlez) For joint pain, Humulene terpene is excellent, present in strains such as Girl Scout Cookies and White Widow I strongly recommend you to read about terpenes and their true power in cannabis. Hope this has helped you a little or at least gave you some lights of this lovely cannabis world.

Many people find CBD to be more relaxing than THC. While CBD is a cannabinoid like THC, it exerts its effects through different mechanisms of action. Research has shown that CBD can affect the serotonin system and the ECS in ways that may promote relaxation. For example, levels of the endocannabinoid AEA are correlated with feelings of relaxation, and CBD has been shown to elevate levels of the AEA by inhibiting the enzymes that break it down. You can incorporate CBD into your relaxation practice in lots of creative ways. For example, you could massage a CBD balm into your feet before taking a relaxing walk. Or you could take a CBD oil capsule before your meditation session. Or you could take a few puffs of a high CBD strain before taking a bath. Both full-spectrum CBD and isolate CBD bolster your endocannabinoid system and can promote feelings of relaxation. Start low and you can always add. Cannabis isn’t a cure all and talking to a therapist would be a great addition.

Put the canning rack in the instant pot or a rag to keep the jar off direct contact with the bottom of the instant pot. Put two to three cup of water in the pot. Seal it up and run it on manual high pressure for 40 min. At the end of 40 min you have decarbed cannabis. Optional put 2-3 tpbs of grain alcohol in the cannabis and shake well. Leave open for a few hours to allow someone of the alcohol to evaporate. Put 1 cup coconut oil in the jar. You can add soy or sunflower letchin to the oil which keeps the tech better suspension in oil. If you skip this just mix Oil before each use. Repeat the process for 40 min. High pressure, no keep warm, natural release. When this is done you have strong THC oil. Strain the cannabis out of the oil. Several methods work like cheese cloth, coffee filters, or a coffee press. After you squeeze the last of the oil out set the oil aside. Set the cannabis a cup or two of boiling water. This will release the remaining oil into the water. Careful dose is impossible to determine or guess for how much THC is in the water Use a dosing calculator to determine how much to use to get the effects you want. It is polite to test it yourself before sharing.

I’m in the middle of a long-ass process of teeth-pulling, abscess-healing, bone-grafting, titanium-screw-inserting, and eventually caps/crowns/something looking like real teeth on top. (I had my bone graft in November, and just got my titanium screws in mid-May, so I had to restart healing process all over again from scratch really recently.) I didn’t smoke/vape at all once I knew the surgical procedure was scheduled and upcoming, I switched to edibles with CBD in them as well. I’m probably rare, but since I had follow up appointments scheduled so frequently post-op each time, and never quite knew if I might get cleared for the next surgery sooner rather than later, or might need a revision of the bone graft, or might have to wait longer than we thought (because of slower than expected healing), it was just better all around to stay off vaping and stay on the edibles. I don’t entirely know why smoking and vaping are bad/worse post-op and while healing, when the same active ingredients so to speak are okay in edibles- maybe it’s literally just the smoke/vapor in the mouth interferes with the healing/microhealing happening in the mouth. Maybe it’s the sucking motion, similar to how straws are banned too. Maybe it’s both or something else or whatever. What is 10 days, compared to risking everything you’ve gone through so far, and the rest of the process that’s left (whatever it is that is planned out, if anything)? If edibles are still too strong for you after however many days post-procedure you are (20 days?), then take smaller edibles? Cut them? You can , but it's wasteful and ineffective. First off: CBD is a hydrophobic molecule, so it will not mix with water and instead will repel. This means the majority of CBD will cling to the surface of the tub, or float on the surface of the water. Second, transdermal absorption of CBD requires dwell time, and given that your body will be largely submerged in water you won't be achieving sufficient dwell time. Third, the average bath tub will hold 80 gallons of water with around 10 square feet of surface area, so as Khyron mentions there's a real issue with dilution. I'd strongly recommend putting that isolate into a balm or topical product that you can massage into your skin and leave there, so it's able to achieve proper dwell time.

>What is the best way to take cbd? I've been mostly taking it in the form of tea, now im trying the cookies. Coincidentally, tea is one of the worst ways lol. CBD is a hydrophobic molecule, so it repels water. When ingested, a very high percentage of the total amount of CBD is destroyed by your liver prior to absorption. Teas/edibles also typically come with a premium due to the convenience and novelty aspects, so while they are, technically, viable dosing methods, the high cost and low bioavailability are considerable drawbacks. I'd recommend using a tincture. The dosage flexibility and accuracy is incredibly useful, sublingual bioavailability is quite a bit higher than ingestion, and it's one of the most cost-effective methods of dosing. Regarding dosage, this varies wildly from person to person, so you'll just want to start low and increase gradually until you achieve desired results. What works for one person may be far too much/little for the next.

Full Spectrum is the stuff that contains THC. It's usually in small enough amounts that it doesn't make people high, but some people are more sensitive than others, so it's up to you whether or not you want to take that risk. Broad Spectrum is pretty good too - it contains supporting chemicals like other cannabinoids, terpenes, and flavonoids. Edibles can be easy to start out with, but difficult to dose more specifically. They're also filled with other ingredients and sugars, and might be more expensive in the long run. Oils are a bit easier to custom dose, and are a bit more bioavailable (more CBD is absorbed by your body). It kind of comes down to personal preference though. In terms of finding products, the best thing to look out for is a public certificate of analysis, or COA. These documents usually contain things like the product's chemical makeup and things like heavy metal and bacteria levels. This transparency is a pretty good thing since there are some sketchy products out there. Good luck! I hope you're able to find something that helps!

pre heat your oven to 250F on a baking sheet spread about 1 ounce of flower or 4 ounces of trim and lay a sheet of foil over it. place the baking tray of bud into the over for 30 mins. this process is called decarbing where we are getting the THC to activate. while this is going, get 16oz of olive oil or coconut oil and 2 cups of water and bring it to a light simmer in a small pot. VERY LIGHT SIMMER. once the buds are done, let them cool for 5 minutes before adding into the oil. now simmer the bud in the oil and water mixture for 4 - 5 hours stirring occasionally. keep the pot at a low bubble. after the time is up, strain the stuff through a wire mesh bowl thing (wholy bowl) cheese cloth might work as well. you can use this oil for baking, cooking or whatever. I like to drizzle my oil over tapenade. stays good in the fridge for about 30 days. edit: put the oil in a glass container, refrigerate, and discard the excess water on top once is solid

I've been using CBD consistently for 6 years now, with virtually no breaks, so here's my quick weigh-in: >long term effects on memory and anxiety No effects to speak of, aside from the anxiety relief provided by dosing CBD. I continue to use THC products so my memory is affected, but I'm confident that's caused by the THC, rather than CBD. >bodily smell by daily consumption None to speak of, and I am (no idea why) unusually sweaty at times, so thank goodness lol. >The right form/dosage for CBD I primarily use full-spectrum tincture, but I keep edibles/capsules on-hand for dosing on-the-go, as well as distillate vape products and hemp flower. In terms of dosage, I'm using 150mg of a tincture with a fairly narrow cannabinoid profile, dosed twice per day. Appropriate dosage varies wildly, though, so I need to stress that my taking 150mg has no bearing on what dosage will work for you, and the cannabinoid profile of the specific product will affect this as well (along with terpenes, if present). On this topic you'll want to brush up on bioavailability, duration of effects, and onset of effects to decide which form is right for you. For example, the bioavailability (percentage of a substance that is absorbed by the body) of ingested CBD (swallowed straight - not to be confused with sublingual \[under the tongue\]) is significantly lower than that of inhalation. The duration of effects, however, is significantly longer when ingested. Anyway, that should get you started, but please feel free to let me know if you're wondering about anything else.

The How to: I rough chopped up the weed i wanted to use, can use shake or flower....doesnt matter. I put it in a mason jar and into my oven at 250degrees. After 30 mins, i take the jar out and open the lid to release pressure. If you do all the opening outside, your house wont smell of weed. Lid back on, shake it up, back in the oven for 30 mins. Once that time is up, open it up and add your oil. Weed to oil ratio will effect strength. My oil is for topical use only so i dont know the strength or percentage. Once the oil is in the jar, back in the oven. 30 mins then open it...back in the oven. After 30 mins, open and then i let it sit on my counter over night. In the morning, i strain it through cheesecloth. I like to squeeze the remnants and get every drop, people who eat this coconut oil say squeezing gives it a VERY strong weed taste. I like to put the oil in a squeeze bottle, like for catchup. Makes it easy to use. ENJOY!

I’ve had COVID 3 times. I’ll give you just a bit from my experiences (American). On my first go around (February 2020), I couldn’t get into a hospital. I was left to do the COVID thing on my own. Had a fever for several days on the initial ramp up. It started with a dry cough that turned into a deep cough. My lungs were in pain after day 5. Every breath hurt and this continued on for the next 2 weeks. Body aches were the worst I’ve had in life. Just felt like I had done a full body work out every day. I had heard that inflammation seemed to be the culprit of a lot of the deaths happening already. They couldn’t keep the inflammation down in the patients. I decided to try a CBD vape that I had because I thought direct application of an anti inflammatory might help (this was a scary choice to make, I’ll be honest. I was sure that I was gonna die anyways so I decided to try it). I also decided to try an Albuterol nebulizer (had one from my son when he was a baby). Albuterol is a bronchial steroid used to open up your airways. The CBD eased the pain in my chest. For the first time I could breath and it not feel like hot ashes in my chest. The Albuterol helped with the coughing. I continued to use both twice a day. 5 puffs on the vape, and the recommended usage of Albuterol. Within a week the pain subsided from my chest, cough lingered for an additional 4ish weeks. December 2020, I get COVID a second time (CBD didn’t prevent me from getting COVID in my experience). This time I was prepared. In the in between time inhad started making my own CBD edibles (120mg CBD, 10 CBG, 7 THC). I took one every day of my time with COVID. I never developed the serious cough from my first experience. I did still have an annoying dry cough, but a tremendous improvement. This bout with COVID left me exhausted. Body aches were present for the first full week. I couldn’t get enough sleep, but my overall symptoms were much milder. I was sick for 2 weeks. Did not have energy to even sit up some days. I lost my taste for 3 days during this experience. I got vaxxed May 2021, boosted Nov 2021 Third times a charm! Beginning of this month (August 2022), wasn’t feeling well. Figured I’d double check just to be sure, but I had COVID. I’ve been back on a smoke kick and I’ve been smoking probably an ounce a week of CBD bud (again, CBD in my experience, doesn’t not prevent COVID). I vaped the 3rd and 4th day of COVID, and then went right back to smoking. This go around I was out for exactly 7 days. Most days, I got up, moved around. Went outside to get sun. By the end, I’d have bursts of energy and clean things around the house and stuff but tried to take it easy. Cough was present but never developed past a dry cough. Body aches were minimal this go around as well. Because I’m vaxxed and boosted, it’s hard to say what CBD contributed to my most recent experience. My experience is completely anecdotal, but I did want to leave it here as a part of the discussion. Let’s break down usage while we’re at it: - Vape - 3mg per puff/as needed and no less than 5 puffs - Homemade Edible - 120mg per/once a day - Smoke - 510mg a day (3 King rolls on average a day @ 170mg CBD a roll)

Just don’t make the mistake of taking these numbers to heart. So like once you do go buy real edibles don’t think cuz you ate one of these 400mg bags that you can take 400mg of real edibles. Recipe for disaster. If they’re from Cali grey market, they’re likely around 1/10th the potency from what I’ve heard on average. But they just lay all the candy out and spray distillate so it can vary dramatically. If you didn’t know you can buy legitimate D9 THC edibles from hemp vendors now a days. They use the entire weight of the gummy and can add up to .3% THC by weight. So every 1gram of edible weight they can legally add 3mg of THC. There’s tons of 5-10-20mg gummies and such out there. Average price from what I’ve seen is around $20 for a 200mg pack of them. Which honestly I’d say would be well worth it if you’re buying these right now. Not throwing shade just being honest. Also it’s stupid easy to make edibles at home, likely much cheaper than even the hemp D9 gummies. Just take your bud, throw in a mason jar loosely tighten lid. Heat in oven at 220f for 25 min, add in coconut oil and sunflower lecithin(optional but recommended) at a 10:1 ratio, tighten lid throw back in the oven at 220f for 45 minutes, strain out the bud using an old cotton tshirt or cheese cloth and you’re good to go. If you get decent bud estimate it at 20% total THC aka 200mg per gram. If it’s a bit mid just say like 12% aka 120mg per gram. Unless you know the exact potency then obviously use that. But for most people this will be by far the cheapest way to go about it. Plus you’ll get a much better high than these distillate based sprayed candy edibles. The hemp gummies are usually pretty decent though, as they typically have a good bit of CBD and other minor cannabinoids which helps balance out the effects and gives a better more fulfilling high.

I'm using a Geekvape m100. Just so you understand, I have just started doing this, so my method is likely to change as I'm a bit of a perfectionist. I make a tincture(master Wu's method. You can do a slow cooker method too, Wu's is just the fastest. Fuck waiting months for it), add it to some propylene glycol (I did a 60/40 mixture, respectively), heat it at a low temp (same you would use to make the tincture), and stir it up really good for about 10 minutes. Assuming you're doing a small batch of tincture and not reducing it to further improve potency, you'll end up with about 45-60 ml of tincture, which you would then add to some vegetable glycerin (all the information I saw said to use propylene glycol with the glycerin at like a 50/50 mix to preserve flavor, but I elected not to use it for personal reasons).

This is something ive learned to do myself over the last few years. The oil at the top of your carts will collect in the seals and little nooks and crannies at the top. I used to use a lighter to melt down this oil in the past but that can burn carcinogens into your oil, which is no bueno. The absolute best way to smoke the most out of your oil carts is to put them in a plastic bag and submerge them in boiling water for a few seconds. This works with almost any cart, but there are a few notes to make; Both plastic and glass will work, but disposables run the risk of ruining the battery from the heat. Never do this to new carts or disposables that you are not absolutely desperate for. This is mainly for heating up the oil inside so that it can move into the cotton and be used. Some cartridges come with terps or other oils that stick to the surface or leave a layer at the top, giving it the illusion that theres still stuff inside. There isnt. If you cannot see the oil collecting after submersion for 10 seconds, then its probably not good oil. Also, since you are heating up the oil, it can leak, very easily. This is another reason to only use empty or close to empty carts and for short periods of time, as large amounts of oil can leak and get wasted if they get too hot. TLDR: Put them in a plastic bag and dip them in hot water.

When you're smelling flower you are smelling a combination of terpenes (the profile) but as soon as you ignite that flower wether it be with fire or by heat or other heat source - you start losing terpenes. Some get burnt away because of the heat. Some get utilized. Terpenes are volatile compounds who degrade even at room temperature which is why proper storage is so important. THC starts to get released around 225°F but fire is much hotter than that. Terpenes start to evaporate/degrade at like 60°F. If you use a dry herb vape you'll notice a huge difference in flavor profile and can play with temperature settings to find your own sweet spot. I make my own wax and use heat to customize my terpene profiles to ones that are enjoyable to me.

Does it make any difference when ingested? Heat won't change this, but sublingual > ingestion in terms of bioavailability. You might already know this, but ingesting (swallowing) CBD results in a large portion of it being destroyed by the liver prior to absorption, whereas sublingual allows you to absorb the CBD prior to ingestion. So to maximize you'd want to sublingual dose first, then swallow the oil.

Several things:. Vaping vs smoking is different on many levels. When you smoke- you are combusting plant material at a very high temperature. That high temp actually burns off many of the terpenes and other cannabinoids before they're useful in our lungs. Vaping allows you to bring more of the plants "entourage" effects into play - at lower temperatures. Depending on the strain and the temp you cook it at, can definitely bring out those qualities (sleepiness). Try vaping at lower temps (like in the 360 range and climb the temp per session to see what suits you). Good luck!

There’s a lot to unpack for you here, boss. Setting aside the relationship issue (which I firmly believe must be solved before any kind of daily medication regimen becomes a part of your treatment) I think you should try marijuana partially set aside from the “treatment” element first. Get your card, head to the shop, buy a small multi-pack of short joints. Usually sold in half gram quantities per joint, this is a great way to start without much hassle or concern. Weed is strong, you don’t need to smoke it all in one go, and you can always have more later. Once you’re properly baked, go find your favorite album from your teenage or school years. I’m sure you have one that you know from front to back, all of the lyrics, every single note. Listen to that with some headphones and as few distractions as you can manage. You’ll almost certainly find something new. Do be sure to prepare yourself before you start. If you have concerns about being away from responsibilities for your family for 4-6+ hours, those will manifest themselves as paranoia and anxiety during your high. This is easily managed but for someone unaware of “how long will this last??” It can be mind-numbingly terrifying for a bit. Once you’ve had a few good sessions you’ll have a much better idea of potency you’re looking for and the dosage you think would be most effective for you. Edibles are quick, easy way to dose that can be extremely consistent, however they are relatively “slow” to work. This can be a bonus for your situation, I feel. Check out some of the low-dose gummies at the store, usually sold between 2-5mg/piece. Those are great for minor dosage in a consistent treatment regimen.

I hate to share this info in the context of your post, but it's also very important: there is no such thing as thc-free hemp flower. All hemp flower contains some amount of THC, or THCa. THCa converts to THC when decarboxylated (vaped, smoked, etc.). You might not need to avoid THC entirely if you responded well to this, but please be cautious. On more than one occasion I've found myself inadvertently high off federally-legal hemp flower. I've been using high-THC cannabis from a dispensary almost daily for the past couple years, so I wouldn't at all say my tolerance is low. Granted, this will depend very much on the cannabinoid profile of the flower you're using, and how much you're using at a time. I'd strongly recommend sticking with broad-spectrum products overall, just to be safe. Broad-spectrum distillate can be bought very cheap and either vaporized or turned into a tincture/oil. There is no such thing as broad-spectrum hemp flower (yet).

I feel this!!!! Tbh, at least in my case, I feel like CBD is partly to blame (false sense of security). The other part is inconsistent meal patterns. I’m also semi-sensitive to THC . TL;DR - skip to next paragraph to read my story LOL! . It appears that limited evidence suggests that CBD doesn’t necessarily have a solid impact on reducing the kick of the high. Actually, when the THC:CBD ratio is 5:1 - 3:1, it appears to slightly increase the intensity, even though it also tends to reduce the anxiety/paranoid/depersonalization effects a bit. In THC:CBD ratios around 2:1 - 1:2 it appears to smooth out the negative effects some, to include mental clarity, but doesn’t really impact intensity. Around the 1:4 - 1:5 range, it appears to strongly mitigate negative effects and provides some blunting effect on intensity, though it’s still pretty psychoactive at this point. This is also where it seems to start becoming slightly sedating. It really isn’t until we get to about 1:10 where it seems like the psychoactive intensity is significantly reduced (though obviously not completely thus the entourage effect) and the risk of the aforementioned negative effects is mostly mitigated, though at 10:1 most edibles will be moderately sedating, although this will depend on the amount of THC to boot. I question whether 25:1 is psychoactive at all and from what I can tell, even medical resources seem to consider this dose non-psychoactive, except in sensitive people who might still feel something. Sadly, research and even expert opinions on my comments seems limited, so others may draw different conclusions. Now for my story lol. The wife has an insanely high tolerance. We live in Canada so get RSO in bulk for very cheap. The most I have seen her eat in one go was 10 grams. What a load of bullshit on their part! The law was probably intended to counteract THC concentrates, but the letter of the law catches gummies too if they are MJ derived. So 4 grams of THC itself can = a bunny with 0.3% THC by weight and they count it as x grams of THC…..so even 1 gummy, usually around 4 grams, is considered a felony. I think Texas is one of the few states this phenomenon happens in. My understanding is that police in certain towns/counties actually make seizing even personal use amounts of THC a high priority of the law! Ridiculous in this era, as even most conservative states consider THC simple possession a low enforcement priority. The excuse they use is drug cartels but given how much is grown and produced in the states, their THC/MJ laws are severely outdated. Not sure if they did already, but you’ll want to be sure the gummies were lab tested to show limited THC content (I assume 10mgs). Even MJ derived, 0.3% or less could be a mitigating factor and most 10mg gummies are around 0.2-0.3%. I hope this helps and good luck!

Quick edible- get half a bit of bread or half a tortilla, anything bread like. I smoked habitually on a daily basis for roughly 15 years, I took college finals stoned, went to work high, drove around, helped friends move, exercised, helped my parents remodel a house they were looking to sell, etc. I smoked all day, every day & didn't let it get in my way until it was in the way & made the choice to put it down for what I thought would be temporary. After taking a long break for a good job opportunity, about 8 months, I just didn't enjoy it the same way when I tried it again. My tolerance was very low after that & when I would have some, vape, smoke, or an edible, it just wasn't fun or relaxing the way it used to be. I also experienced the anxiety / panic I'd heard so many people describe but never encountered myself before & that was very off-putting to say the least. My responsibilities in life also changed over time while I wasn't smoking. I started a family & have people relying on me to provide financially & medical benefits. Even though I'm in a state where cannabis is legal recreationally, I'm in an industry & at a position where I could be fired for partaking. A couple times when I tried getting high I wound up worried about what happens if I were to get a random drug test & was looking at jobs all night saving positions I could apply to, not a great way to relax & that's one of the things I miss about it is it used to be a great way to relax. I tried low THC, high CBD & it didn't seem to make that big of a difference, once I crossed the line I'd worry & start looking at jobs. Not fun times. Maybe when I'm retired & don't have anything to worry about, or if the federal laws ever change & my place of employment changes their policies I'll try it again, otherwise, no interest. In those moments where getting high would have been a good way to relax, a strong drink or two in proper moderation can do the trick but when it's high stress over a long period of time & you don't want to build a tolerance to alcohol & have a few every night, that's where you really miss that aspect of cannabis but there are other ways to decompress & disconnect from work stress. If you’re looking to avoid intaking THC, avoid smoking or vaping any CBD products. Small quantities of CBD get converted into THC via pyrolysis when it is heated up to be vapourised or smoked, and it does produce a psychoactive effect albeit milder than products with actual THC content. Broad spectrum should be fine if you’re taking it orally or sublingually, the trace amounts of THC are typically low enough that you shouldn’t notice any type of impairment unless you’re taking heroic megadoses. Full spectrum I can’t really speak about as I live in the UK and all our CBD extracts can’t contain any meaningful amount of THC. Not sure about the US and other countries where THC is looked upon more favourably by the law, you may find the levels can be high enough to have an effect, but generally if they’re listed as <0.1% or thereabouts, you shouldn’t have an issue.

I haven't had a lot of experience with CBD flower, so that might impact my thoughts. However, as someone who's struggled with a lot of anxiety, I find oils/capsules can really help me. Personally, CBD doesn't fix my anxiety, but taking a bit of CBD oil helps my mind calm down a little and things feel a lot less panicked. I've done a bunch of vaping, which I like because it hits faster, but the effects seem to go away quicker too. So oils, I find personally, are a bit more consistent. One thing to keep in mind if you're thinking about swapping is that there is a bit of difference in effect. Smoking/vaping kicks in a lot faster, though the effects can also wear off a bit quicker too. It's also more bioavailable (more is absorbed by your body), so you may find that you need to adjust your dose if you're trying out oils or capsules. There's also a difference between oils and capsules. Oils are generally sublingual, or get absorbed under the tongue. Which makes them kind of middle-of-the-road in terms of bioavailability, fast acting, and how long the effects last. Capsules are the same as edibles - so less fast acting and bioavailable. All that is to say, with oils/capsules, I find you have to do a bit more planning ahead and be a bit more consistent with doses. If you do want to try them out (and I say go for it), just be prepared to experiment a bit with dose, because it'll probably be different than smoking was for you. Also, taking high amounts of CBD is relatively safe - according to the science we have at the moment. So I might take oils to give me those more consistent effects but then vape a little to kind of supplement. What works for me might not work for you, but there's a lot you can kind of play around with. Good luck!

1. When THC and CBD are consumed together, they can have a synergistic effect, meaning that the combination of the two can be more effective than either one alone. When THC and CBD are consumed together, they can enhance and modulate each other's effects. CBD can reduce some of the negative side effects associated with THC, such as anxiety, paranoia, and memory impairment. At the same time, THC can enhance the therapeutic effects of CBD, such as its ability to reduce pain and inflammation. Additionally, CBD can moderate the psychoactive effects of THC, making for a more balanced, less intense high. It's worth noting that the ratio of CBD and THC can greatly affect the experience you have when consuming the both together. Different strains will have different ratios, and everyone's body responds differently. 2. For the effectiveness of CBD for sleep and anxiety, CBD helps promote relaxation and reduce feelings of anxiety and stress, which in turn helps with sleep. So yes, it really does help. 3. As for the consumption method, both oils and vaping have their own benefits. CBD oil is generally considered to have a longer lasting effect, while vaping may provide more immediate relief. Make your own RSO edibles. I make 125 10 to 15mg gummies for about 55 bucks ( I shop RSO down to 25 per syringe or so). 4 tablespoons of unflavored (like knox, I use bone gel) gelatin. 1 tablespoon lecithin. 1 cup of pure fruit juice (mmm cherry) . Mold inhibitor if they will stay around longer than a month and unrefrigerated. 1/2 cup juice and 2 tblsp in a bowl to bloom. the other 1/2 cup juice and 2 tblsp to a low simmer. Remove from heat. Blend in the other 2 tablespoons gelatin, add the lecithin, add two RSO syringes pour into mold. Refrigerate after about half an hour remove and enjoy. I make this recipe three time a month. This process takes 15 minutes plus refrig time.

Repeated and frequent use. Edible tolerance goes up way quicker than smoking tolerance. When you smoke/vape, you are high for 2-3 hours, when you eat an edible, you are high for 4-8 hours, the longer you are high for, the faster your tolerance increases. Tinctures even moreso cause they get you high for 8-12 hours. Its also technically a different chemical, when you smoke, the THC molecule is absorbed by your lungs and then binds with the cb1 receptor, when you eat an edible, it is processed by your liver and it turns into 11-hydroxy THC, which is treated differently by the cb1 receptor. So you can have a high edible tolerance and a low smoking tolerance, or vice versa.

My dude obviously hasn't tried TCH-P, now mind you I really need a CBD tincture to go along with this as it's so potent. TCH-P aka Tetrahydrocannabiphorol aka (-)-Trans-Δ9-tetrahydrocannabiphorol has far more endocannabinoid receptor activating bits on them, this makes it about 7-10 times as potent as normal Δ9 THC. The actual binding affinity is 33x higher but the actual psychedelic effect is 10x. It's also naturally occuring in some strains of sataiva plants and it's thought that some strains are lauded for their potency despite having the same or even lower percentages of Δ9 THC compared to other strains due to their THC-P content. It's found in the seeds of almost all strains of cannabis.

Marijuana is an amazing plant. Not just because it can help with so many maladies but also because of the safeguards that are built into it. One of these is the near impossibility to over dose and the other is the increased tolerance to THC over time as you use it. At some point your body is not capable of feeling it anymore. In turn you need to use more and more to get the same effect, especially where recreational use is concerned. This helps prevents long term over use and encourages users to take breaks in order to reset their bodies to accepting THC again. Eventually the user will get bored of the low effectiveness and high cost and stop using it. It is no indication of addiction as THC is not chemically addictive. It is merely habitual like phone. Even candy (sugar) is more chemically addictive than weed. While less dangerous than chemical addiction, it's still bothersome. You don't need to throw out your stash, just take a week off. Theraputic THC doses such as those found in RSO (Rick Simpson Oil) are quite high and require the user to gain increased toleration. The high strong buzz eventually becomes not a big deal, even at high doses and becomes very much therapeutic instead of recreational for a buzz and a good time. If you are concerned, perhaps find a different and more effective type and delivery method that works best for your requirements. RSO for example is best taken as a suppository and lasts much longer. Users often take large doses but not for a buzz. Maybe the doses required to maintain your therapy needs are too much to simply smoke? You may need to try edibles or extracts if the inhaling of smoke is either insufficient to give you effects or too much clorophyll laden buds to be burning all the time. Bottom line: You are very likely not addicted just habitual and have a higher theraputic need. No need to throw your stash away, just take a break and/or change method or dose to accommodate. I highly recommend walking, draining your batteries and stretching deeply before bed and when you wake. Honestly address your diet and activity. They are pivotal to your good sleep.. I'm a normal guy who's been safely at this for 35+ years. I also studied sleep professionally for nearly a decade. Take care. Hope this helps. Sleep well.

Anything that has a smell or taste has terpenes. It’s the chemical compounds that make up smells and tastes of everything. Every strain has a unique terpene profile, but many strains have a lot of the same terpenes in different concentrations. Limonene, Pinene, Myrcene, Linalool are a few of the main ones you will see. You can probably guess the flavors and smells of the first two by the name, Myrcene is in mangos and Linalool is in Lavender. There’s over 400 known terpenes in cannabis, but only a handful are ever in high enough concentration to impact the smell and flavor of the flower. Smoking a bowl, bong, joint are all going to burn the cannabis the same way and produce similar effects in varying intensities and durations. There are several extraction methods for making cartridges, some of which completely separate the THC from everything else , including terpenes. High THC distillate, which is the cheapest form of extract, may be upwards of 80% THC, and that’s because everything else has been stripped out so your literally left with the THC and excess lipids that can’t be removed. Some manufacturers will add terpenes back into distillate, either derived from the actual cannabis strain, or from other plants, which is where it gets shady since there’s no science on consuming non-cannabis derived terpenes. Other extractions like wax and shatter use a solvent like butane to do something similar, but it is not as effective at separation which still leaves other cannabinoids and terpenes in the final product. Live resin and live rosin both use the plant that is freshly frozen right after it is cut down to preserve it in a “live” state, which many believe preserves more terpenes and cannabinoids. Live rosin only uses water as a solvent, which is why some people will say live rosin is a “solvent-less” extract, as they don’t count water as a solvent. Because there are no other chemicals involved in this process, and the process of making live rosin is more natural, you can preserve the most terpenes and cannabinoids of any extraction method for what is called a “full-spectrum extract”, encompassing the full spectrum of the plants profile. This is also why rosin is the most expensive concentrate. Long story short, every extract is made differently and will produce different results. Ever strain is unique and will produce different results. Every smoking method is relatively similar, but will still produce slightly different results. For “body effects” like you want, you need to focus on CANNABINOIDS , not terpenes. And that’s a whole other can of worms. Edit: since everybody is asking about cannabinoids, I’ll admit up front I know less about them than terpenes. But that goes for pretty much everybody! There’s over 100 known cannabinoids in cannabis, the two most prevalent being THC and CBD. Other more common cannabinoids that you’ll see these days are CBG and CBN as well. You can probably guess from the name that cannabinoids are exclusive to cannabis, but that actually isn’t true either. Black pepper, Echinacea, and even Sunflowers have cannabinoids! Don’t ask me what they are cus idk lol. But, our brains have specific receptors designed to connect with cannabinoids when they are released in the body, which is why people believe that humans have had a symbiotic relationship with cannabis and cannabinoids for centuries. It’s literally part of us. Cannabinoids don’t impact flavor or smell, but more effects on the body. Yes, terpenes do also have effects that may be similar, but to a lesser extent. Everybody knows THC is the main cannabinoid, it gets you high, it’s the good stuff. CBD is most prevalent in hemp and exploded in 2018 after the federal government legalized hemp. “Legal hemp” is any part of the cannabis plant, seed or extract that contains less than .3% THC on a dry weight basis. Another can of worms there . People like CBD for its relaxing effects, it helps with muscle recovery, anxiety, etc. which is why it’s the most popular aside from THC. CBN and CBG can provide similar benefits to varying intensities. But like I said, we know even less about cannabinoids than terpenes, so there’s still a lot to learn. Also just a note to everyone thinking I’m some expert; I’ve been writing about cannabis since 2013, and I’ve worked in the legal industry since 2017. Everything I know I’ve learned from just doin some google searches and learning from my peers. Just because what I say may sound accurate (and I do believe it is!), doesn’t mean you should take it as gospel and move on. Do your own research, learn about the plant. I started as a basic consumer that just wanted to know what I was smokin on the black market. It evolved into a career for me. I’ve learned more about this plant than I ever thought was even possible, because I just wanted to know . The information is out there, you don’t even have to ask on Reddit. Be curious. You’ll find some shit that might flip your perception of cannabis on its head.

So I worked at a cannabis grow that had multiple phd peeps doing studies. Clones of a plant grown under as identical conditions as you can make it can have extremely varying levels of thc, cbd, terpenes, etc. This happened for all indicas, sativas, and hybrids. My take away from it is that labels and % mean nothing. The best way to know what’s in what you’re smoking is to lookup the lab result on the specific batch of oil you’re using (not possible with flower because 1 buds result’s will be used to label quite a few others). Then trial and error to see how certain high levels of different terpenes tend to make you feel, then continue buying oils that as closely matches your the ones you liked the most.

You definitely need to decarb first. Get a proper mason jar/lid (no plastic) so you can decarb in the same jar that you process the mixture in. My process for freezer tincture: Put Everclear in freezer. Decarb finely ground weed in jar with lid on. Let jar cool to room temp, then put it in the freezer for 12ish hours. Add the “frozen” Everclear to the jar until the bud is full covered plus another half inch. Less is better imo so your solution is more concentrated. Leave everything in the freezer for a week. Shake the jar 3 times a day. Strain through cheesecloth, strain through coffee filter, bottle into tincture droppers, enjoy your extremely efficient (there is no better way to extract the maximum amount of chemicals from flower) full-spectrum edibles.

First of all, a topical won't penetrate into your bloodstream. It's topical, which means it sits on the top of your dermis (skin) and will not penetrate through the layers to even reach the bloodstream. Tropicals are good for immediate relief for pain that is close to the skin. This includes muscle strains and areas of arthritis. The cream for your hands is fine, and you can apply as much as you want. The relief begins at 1-5 minutes and can last up to two hours. But since it's on your hands you will probably have to reapply often. You cannot overdose on a topical. If your pain is super severe, topical will give you instant relief, but won't last long so you'll be reapplying it a lot. Second of all, you didn't specify what kind of gummies you're taking but I'll assume it's also CBD. The gummies will take an hour or so to feel any positive effects but since you have to metabolize it, you lose some of the effect. That should last anywhere in your system as pain relief (like a Tylenol effect) for up to 4-6 hours. Depending on when you take the gummy, the effect may weer off before you notice it's done it's job (like at night). You cannot overdose on CBD. Let me repeat that. You cannot overdose on CBD. You might have a point where you take too much and you might feel agitated or restless (and in your case I'm only referring to the gummy, but you'd have to take SEVERAL gummies at once). Everyone's body is different but I guarantee if your pain is as bad as you're implying in the original post, you're most definitely not taking in enough CBD and not often to keep the pain relieved, or your CBD isn't reputable, or you're not giving it time to work. I suggest you go into a reputable CBD store and talk to the people that work there. A good store will have trained STAFF, and reputable product to be able to guide you through the multiple ways to take CBD, dosage guides, effects, etc. I work in one of those places. In my personal opinion, you should be looking into a Full Spectrum TINCTURE to add to your regimen. The tincture onset is faster (gets into bloodstream via sublingual), and more effective since you don't digest it like a gummy that loses bioavailability. The relief lasts the same (4-6 hours). Your local CBD shop will have a good variety of options: isolate, broad, full spectrum, strengths (the amount of MG in each bottle) and literature or education on all of their product. On top of the, Google is your friend and SO MUCH of the questions people have can be answered with simple searches, then you don't have to go into the CBD store blindly.

>Is a purely CBD strain something I should take in your opinion? No such thing exists. All hemp flower contains an array of cannabinoids and terpenes, including THC/THCa, so if you want "purely CBD" then you'll want an isolate product. That said, hemp flower is a perfectly viable option to try as a sleep aid. I've never found CBD or hemp flower to be very effective when compared to traditional aids such as magnesium l-threonate, [properly-dosed]( melatonin, valerian root, etc. I'm not sure that an inhalable is the best route for someone with sleep apnea, and the duration of effects is relatively short (1 hour) compared to a sublingual dosed oil or ingested edible, but seeing as you already have the stuff it's worth a shot. I just wouldn't expect too much, as that small bit of THC/THCa will inhibit REM sleep more per mg than CBD will encourage REM sleep. As flipper mentions, CBN is the sleep cannabinoid. CBD helps mainly if the difficulty sleeping is related to anxiety or inflammation.

Tl;DR Labels use a lot of tricks (and sometimes outright exaggerate real results) to make weed look more potent than it actually is. In short for comparison, about .25g of this weed should be comparable to a 10mg edible. Generally weed labeled over 20 is going to be "good", higher numbers are generally stronger but not always. 15 to 20 expect like average 90s quality (not the best the 90s had to order, the average 8th kind of a thing). Any lower and it's for a light buzz or for people who smoke high volume with joints or blunts. The percentages are typically exaggerated, strong weed these days gets labeled around 30%, but in reality is probably more like 20%, labs tend to fluff it up. That's why some 21% strains feel as strong as 29% strains (Also there is a lot to discuss here about terpenes, but I'll leave it out). One thing to keep in mind about the percentage is it's by weight not volume. You hear 20-30% and you expect that 1/3 of the big nug is pure THC, but THC is denser than the leaf material, so if it's 20% they are saying you have .2 grams of THC per gram. However, they are typically listing THCA content, the acid form of THC, not THC (sometimes it's total cannabinoids, which would include CBD, CBN, CBG, etc, also sometimes in their acid forms). When THCA is converted to THC through heating (decarboxylation) it loses about 20% of its mass. Also, when smoking, you flash off a lot of THC as the plant burns (this is why a quality vaporizer makes your weed last 50%-100% longer, it is destroying less THC). For comparison, let's consider smoking .2 grams of this weed (and this is going to be some real real approximate numbers here). So we have 200 mg of material labeled at 28%, in reality it's probably 23% total that got inflated at the lab, and really more like 20% THCA. So we have .2 200 = 40 mg of THCA. As it's decarboxylated, it loses about 20% of it's mass so we are left with .8 40 = 32 mg THC. Now let's consider that we flash off a good proportion while smoking, somewhere between 25% and 50% (this is an estimate based on my experience using vaporizers and about how much longer my weed lasts, not super precise, but Im a nerd so I do weigh and track everything to compare). Let's say it loses 33% to split the difference. So now we are at .7 32 = 22.4 mg THC. When you consider this, at the end of the day what we actually take in would be closer to 10% in reality which is a more logical number. I think mg in edibles are probably the most understood, those products have consistency issues as well, but I think most people with access to legally produced edibles understand what 10mg feels like. So now someone may think, "Are you trying to tell me that .2 grams of that flower is supposed to be a 22mg edible?". Which yeah, sounds way off. However, when you eat cannabis your liver turns the THC into 11 Hydroxy THC that is somewhere around (don't quote me cause it's debated) 3 times stronger than regular THC. So for our comparison, let's reduce our 22.4 mg to about 7.5 mg of "edible THC". Now it's sounding better, that's saying about a quarter gram of smoked cannabis labeled at 28% is likely the equivalent of about 1 10mg edible. So point is, these labels can tricky. Should mention some are quite good and have much more granular info than others, including terpenes, THCA, CBD, CBN and the estimated true THC after decarboxylation. Some basically just give a total cannabinoid percentage. Depending on State/Province/Country you're in the rules for labeling will be different as well. So you can either nerd the fuck out about it like I did (it's basically a compulsion of mine, so def not a brag lol) or you can follow some rules of thumb: labeled over 25% , unless it's a shit grower, it's gonna be very strong weed. Labeled 20% to 25%, for sure modern day strength weed, perhaps even as strong as the stuff next to it that says 29%, but most likely a little weaker. 15% to 20% your getting like 90s weed (the average person's illegal bag btw, obviously strong weed also existed, but definitely was not readily available). Under 15% is for people who either want very light effects or prefer smoking in volume like joints or blunts.

I agree that you should keep the cart upright for purposes of keeping the wick wet. However, I disagree that this prevents clogging. First - my theory about what causes the clogging is this... The wick is usually touching the coil (some carts are different designs inside), but it is in very, very close proximity to the air passage. I believe that incomplete combustion and either the normal airflow, or more likely, drawing too hard, cause the oil to seep up the sides of the tube. As you hit it a few times, that oil is drawn away from the heating element and it cools faster than it reheats as it slides down the tube, accumulates, and then clogs. I do not believe the mechanism has anything to do with how the oil flows within the tube, and I suspect there is a slight negative pressure inside the oil chamber. FYI - you can clear a clogged cart almost immediately with something long and thin. I use the butt end of a burnt incense stick. Happens to be the perfect size and disposable. I thus do not believe that whether it is upright or on its side really matters. I've never noticed a difference and I go through a lot of carts - like I have 5 at various levels just in sight on my desk. The oil inside will have a varying viscosity, depending on the processing method. Even the lightest oils flow slowly though. But it is clear that it doesn't flow enough to pass through the wick on its own, or else all carts would come off upright shelf display clogged. That also means that being on its side isn't going to cause that to happen either. If anything, gravity assistance would make it more likely in an upright position. IMHO the best way to prevent clogging is to not draw too hard. Let the coil do the work for you and draw a nice thick cloud with slow airflow. You could also run something down the tube regularly.

Ooooh I love terpenes, these are great. Terpenes are in everything that grows. The smell of a pine tree means you’ll have pinene in there. Limonene is the terpene found in lemons, oranges, different citrus fruits and usually a dominant limonene profile means it’s a sativa. Myrcene is the terpene that makes you sleepy/calm, so if that’s the dominant terpene it’s an indica. Linalool is going to be found in lavender. If you really want to geek out and get snobby with your flower, google different terpenes to figure out what they do. When I worked at a dispensary it was explained to me like this: THC is the car. It will get you high, no problem. But Terpenes are the driver to take you to a certain kind of high. They’ll direct how you’re feeling. That’s why when you get vape cartridges, the best thing is to get live resin or full spectrum, because it hasn’t extracted those terpenes. You can get strain specific with those.
Best advice is to go easy and take it real slow, especially with the vape. Enjoy the ride!

If legalized the price drops dramatically over time, which is awesome for consumers-but not necessarily for tax revenue or growers. Here in neighboring Michigan the 5 nearby dispensaries to my home (all on 1 street!) have great cannabis for less than $50 an ounce. For perspective: In Michigan right now all adults can possess up to 2.5 ounces of cannabis on their person or in their car (10oz if locked in the trunk) up to 15 grams of extracts, grow 12 plants with no oversight or restrictions, and most former misdemeanors and felonies for cannabis are now civil infractions or not a crime at all. Proposition 1 is one of the worlds most liberal recreational cannabis legalization laws. There are recreational dispensaries as well as home cannabis delivery services pretty much everywhere here. All this has lead to a huge overabundance of supply resulting in the highest quality cannabis of 25-30% THC selling for $50 an ounce on the black market. Also the liquor stores and convenience stores as well as some gas stations sell marijuana product such as Delta 8 and HHC cannabis flower, pre rolls, and cartridges for vapes. Many low THC CBD products including all that and much more are also sold at many establishments. The overall effect is that profits for criminal enterprises and large commercial growers are on a massive decline which is great in my opinion.

Depends on what type of hemp edibles you were eating 120mg of...in the United States, the 2018 farm bill made it possible for the sale of edibles that contain comparable THC levels to edibles sold across the imaginary lines in the next state in even prohibition states, like Wisconsin. As long as it is sourced from hemp flower, defined as <0.3% THC, it's legal to sell. So if you were getting any delta 8 or 9 combined with CBD/CBG/CBC etc, it'll be a different experience. I'm aware of the subreddit we're in, but thought it'd be worthy of throwing out there. However, if you are used to hemp edibles that were not psychoactive, you can expect the differences that outlined. Combustion will give more of a head high from absorption via lungs as well as the oxygen deprivation that happens simultaneously and you'll feel the full force v quickly compared to the edible creep. Dry vaping flower allows for better control over enjoyment of terpenes; it's even milder than taking a lighter to it, but a healthier and more cost effective (already vaped bud/decarboxylated for making your own edibles/infusions) option.

My father in law suffers from Parkinson's and a combination of 1:1 CBD and THC work wonders for him. He was also getting really depressed. We found this lady (Alice) who gave him a free consultation, and once they determined what was best for him, helped him getting the medicine delivered to us (we live in a state where getting this type of medicine is problematic). She also keeps track of his progress and is always available to talk to him if he feels he needs to modify his dose. Anyway, a 1:1 CBD/THC protocol would be ideal for this. Things to keep in mind: 1. Make sure whatever medicine you are getting does NOT come from hemp. Hemp is a bioaccumulator and is really effective at extracting toxins from the ground. Also, most CBD extracted or isolated from Hemp comes from the byproduct or leftovers from Hemp that has been used for industrial processes. 2. A 1:1 CBD/THC tincture or FECO will not get your mom high. The "antidote" for getting high from THC is CBD, so having a 1:1 ratio would prevent your mom from getting "high". 3. If possible, talk to a specialist so you know you are getting the right medicine from your mom. Reddit is a great place to learn about a lot of things, but ultimately, we are all different and having a specialist assess your mom's situation will be what's best for her. 4. If you chose to move forward with cannabis medicine for you mom, make sure the medicine you get comes from cannabis and not hemp. Also, avoid isolates. Cannabis medicine works best because of the entourage effect. You need all the cannabinoids that come from the cannabis plant for the medicine to be effective. Isolates just won't have such a powerful effect as full molecule medicine will. Those are my 2 cents. Let me know if you'd like me to get you in touch with Alice. SHe's really knowledgeable and her consultations are 100% free. Again, even if you don't buy anything from her, she will help your mom get the best assessment so you can make a better decision. You smoke about 4oz a week? That’s literally wild as fuck Legit not trying to be an ass, but you’d get so much higher if you practiced a bit of moderation. I’ve had all sorts of tolerance levels throughout my years of using, and by far the strongest best highs are always while I keep my tolerance low. After about the 2-3.5gram of flower a day range I’d always reach a point where I’m essentially not getting stoned. Or I’m high constantly, so high just feels normal. Which at that point all smoking more does is give me a little boost for 5-10min after smoking and I’m back down to the general kind of stoned feeling. There’s a reason for this though, when we use THC it fills our cannabinoid receptors causing the high, but they get down-regulated in the process aka “taken offline”. It then takes time for those same receptors to empty and up-regulate aka “UAV back online”. When we keep smoking while having a ton of receptors offline, our bodies start to produce more and more cannabinoid receptors so it has somewhere to put the additional cannabinoids being consumed. This is what tolerance is on a super basic level, it’s from having way more receptors to fill than a non user, and we have to fill them all to get the effects we are after. The good news is it doesn’t take long for our receptors to up-regulate. One study found that after just 24hours of cessation that the stoners had about 50-75% of the open receptors compared to the non using control group, and after just 72hours there was no statistical difference observed. This means a short 1-3 day break every so often would do wonders for a user looking to get stoned, and to help prevent tolerance increases. Of course, the longer the better, so a month break will help way more than a few days. It’s also better to use 1-3 times a day, and each session get throughly stoned vs sitting and smoking smaller amounts throughout the entire day. This is the reason carts can nuke someone’s tolerance quickly, as they lend themselves to constant microdosing, where the person takes a few hits every hour throughout the day or whatever. It’s also why the first smoke of the day smacks so hard compared to the rest, as you’ve given your receptors a solid 8hours to come back. So some basic guidlines and tips I’ve implemented that have helped insane amounts with tolerance and getting the best effects has been by taking a 1-3 day break twice a month. The next one is the 1-3 sessions a day, preferably with a good few hours between each. Another one has been using CBD in my weed. Which there’s a common misconception that any CBD at all will dull the effects of THC. In fact, that’s not the case at all, it’s only when you use a ratio of THC:CBD lower than 1:2 when you’ll even start feeling the CBD affecting the THC. Below a 1:2 ratio CBD has an additive effect, where the high is stronger vs THC alone. There’s multiply studies on this as well. The best effects were fined from the groups that used at around a 2:1 ratio THC:CBD. You can do this by buying CBD dominant hemp flower and mixing it in, or by buying mixed ratio strains. Many other ways as well. CBG has a greater effect at reducing the effects felt from THC, but with the added benefit of significantly slowing tolerance building. All cannabis has at least a little CBG, just like all cannabis has a little THC. So you’d need to add in some CBG dominant stuff into the mix if you wanted to utilize it for this tolerance slowing effect. Just careful with CBG, it stimulates adrenaline release and it’s a big part of the reason some weed feels so racy, especially bud that’s been chopped a bit early. Figured I’d share, as I’m sure many could benefit from this info. It is counterintuitive, you’d think the more you smoke the higher you’d get, but there’s a certain line for all of us that once we cross it, using more will actually make us less stoned overall. At a half Oz a day I’d have to imagine your big into blunts or fat joints, but even if that is the case it’s still well into the area of seeing these diminishing returns. Like I said, it’s happened for myself multiple times once I was in the 2-3.5g out of a bong range. Sorry for the long comment, hope this helps a bit, or at least gives some food for thought. Take care homie!!
