I fully understand the concept of varying dosage and dosage frequency from one individual to another based upon ailment, severity and biomechanics. I will always explain that to folk as I go over initial dosage. We use the phrase "suck it and see" all the time, what works for some folk doesn't necessarily work for all. Try a few till you find the right one. If we're talking oil here, one of the most prominent. We start near enough everyone on a 5mg dose to be used morning and evening and further if required. Whether that's a broad-Spectrum or a full-spectrum content. At the same time, I will explain that some get a great initial response immediately, some not so much and in those cases to stick at it a number of days to let a level build up and kick the system into gear. From there if they don't think they've had the full response, we say to work upwards. Depending on what a customer is in for, we may suggest a higher strength and dosage initially. Typically cancers, we look to stronger oils. However I've got an old ex-speedway racer with crushed vertebrae, slipped disks, broke this, fractured that. Who had spent around £200 on CBD elsewhere, we knew these only used Isolated CBD products at this time, stuck him on a full-spec 5mg dose and it absoloutely spanked anything else. He was in the following day singing it's praises, it was a good one. Many tend to find the 20 - <40mg per dose mark does the trick for them in the end and stick at that. Some may move further if necessary but many hang around there. Whilst I could initially sell them a higher strength, and make a little extra by sticking them straight on a higher dose, if I KNOW that starting dose can be great and it'll keep them happy as it's more cost-efficient too, I'll do that everytime. People already have a concept that it's expensive to begin. When it comes to gummies however, around about 20mg is the starting point so it also varies from product to product. One thing to consider is that the FDA have set guidelines for the recommended CBD intake daily at 70mg in the UK. Supposed liver issues with prolonged use. We've seen the test and know how that conclusion came to be, it's not the case. We've also had way over that ourselves to no issues on occasion and use that as an example you wont be harmed by a high dose if needed. These guidelines however are on every UK CBD labeling over here. This makes it difficult for us as a store to suggest someone take triple digit doses without leaving us in a tricky position. They even recently tried to suggest 10mg be the daily limit. Whilst we explain the 70mg top, I will explain that yourselves in the states use way higher doses so it does act as a good one for helping out there. Appreciate the insight to the thoughts behind the oil, if it is done correctly, then it's a good way to go about it to keep it low-cost.

So you can take products that ONLY have CBD or THC in them, and that’s fine, they will still do their job on their own. But they do work considerably better together as a team. Thats why you’ll see a lot of CBD and THC pairings in dispensaries. Most of them are ratios of 1:1 or 1:1:1 and usually contain either only CBD and another non-psychoactive compound or THC and two or more non-psychoactive compound like CBN or CBG. CBD doesn’t JUST help with anxiety and pain, but those are the most commonly advertised reasons to use it. CBN is a compound that comes from aged cannabis, originally from THC strains. It helps promote deep body relaxation and a good number of people use it for help getting to sleep, myself included on occasion. It’s better than melatonin because it isn’t interfering with your body’s natural melatonin production. CBG is where a lot of your anti-inflammatory, anti-tumor, and anti-nausea properties come from. It’s the best at fighting cancer and its symptoms out of all compounds and can help with conditions with similar symptoms. CBD is kind of all encompassing. It can help with pain, with anxiety, nausea, relaxation, etc. But it can also keep the high feeling from THC in check by reducing the effects of the high up to 60ish percent, so it’s a great addition to a beginner product for someone just getting into edibles or those with a lower tolerance. Quitting weed, especially after long-term use, can be tough, but it's absolutely doable. Here are some tips that might help: 1. Stay Hydrated and Eat Light Foods: To manage nausea, try sipping on ginger tea or peppermint tea - both are known for their soothing effects. Small, bland meals like crackers or rice can also help. 2. CBD as an Alternative: Smoking CBD could help with cravings, but it’s a personal choice. If you go this route, look for high quality flower or oil with no additives. Edibles or tinctures might be gentler on your system. 3. Relaxation Techniques: Anxiety can be rough, so consider mindfulness practices like meditation or deep breathing exercises. Lavender essential oil (not vapes) can also have a calming effect. 4. Non Smoking Alternatives: Herbal blends with chamomile, mullein, or peppermint can mimic the act of smoking without harmful side effects. They’re usually mild and don’t taste like detergent! 5. Distract Yourself: Keep busy with hobbies, exercise, or something creative. The more occupied your mind is, the easier it will be to push past cravings. 6. Talk to Someone: Whether it’s a counselor, support group, or trusted friend, sharing your experience can make the process feel less isolating. 7. Prepare for Sleep Changes: If you have trouble sleeping, try natural remedies like melatonin or valerian root. A solid nighttime routine can also help regulate your sleep cycle. Remember, you're not alone, and it's okay to ask for support. You've got this!

What you're experiencing is super common for people who take long breaks and come back. Modern weed is WAY more potent than it was a decade ago (some strains pushing 30%+ THC), and without tolerance, it can absolutely trigger anxiety and paranoid thoughts. That "hidden truth" feeling is actually just THC amplifying your existing anxieties and making normal things seem suspicious - it's not revealing any real insights. I've seen tons of people get convinced their partners were being shady while high, only to realize the next day they were just catastrophizing normal behavior. My advice? If you want to smoke again, start with ultra-low THC strains (like 5-10%) or mix your regular weed with CBD flower at a 1:4 ratio. Take ONE small hit and wait 15 minutes. The mistake most returners make is treating their tolerance like it used to be.

Hello. I was thinking of writing this question on a thread of its own in some sub, but I don't need tons of people judging me, just one that understands to some degree what I go through. I read your answer and I'm very interested in hearing more. What's your story? Did you ever use any other drug, alcohol? What kind of mental struggles do you have? If it's okay, I'll share some of mine. I didn't have an objectively more traumatic life than others. In fact, it was quite sheltered, the issues I faced only hurt me to the degree that they did due to my sensitivity. This is how I describe myself. Not as having condition A or B, just sensitive. I lean towards depression, anxiety and bipolar. Depression as in feeling like there is no hope. Anxiety as in being overwhelmed by stress to the point of shaking. Lastly, bipolar as in feeling things are too sad or too happy, being euphoric or disphoric. It's mostly defined as a state of a lot of energy, it feels out of control at times, and my instinct is to hide. That's where the psychosis sometimes comes in. In the times it came, it did in moments of very intense anxiety. I differentiate the two because one can get very anxious but won't necessarily feel they are losing their mind, whilst I did or feared I would. I'm talking about feeling reality is not real, or that existence should end. It's very difficult to bare. By staying away from drugs of all kinds and engaging with life, I am okay now. I still deal with anxiety and depression, but everything is manageable. I can survive. But I wanted more. I want to live, not just survive. To enjoy. To seek new job opportunities, be more calm and responsible for my family. Sometimes I feel medication or CBD could help me with that. I also think about drinking wine on occasion. I never drank it. Just some champagne when I was a child and only felt more energized and happy, nothing bad, but things changed. My anxiety and fear have developed I think. Things that my child self could tolerate I find frightening now. I just don't want to panic or be overburdened with a state I do not wish to be in. The first and last time I used CBD tincture, full spectrum, 50mg CBD + 1,5-2mg THC, I got anxious at first. I feared where the substance would take me. I felt death near, but I calmed myself and it got better. I think it only started having a calming effect after 4h or so. It felt like having an airblower blowing wind on my face. I wasn't super relaxed, and I remember wondering if it was worth if it gave me only a small relief. The day ended okay. The hard part was the days following. If she's having to up the dosage then perhaps she's taking the regular dose too often. I'd recommend skipping a day, and going back to 5mg. With microdosing the goal is to find the lowest effective dose, so if you feel you're having to double your dose for the same effect as before, you're going the wrong direction. Also, is she eating anything before taking the edible? If you take one on an empty stomach you may think it should hit harder but then it doesn't, which can lead you to believe you need more. THC binds to fat so try to always take the edible right after eating. And for some edibles I try a half dose first. With live rosin sativa gummies I usually like 2.5 mg, with other hybrids or THC/CBD gummies I like 5mg.
